1:
"Then Jesus, six days before the passover, came to Bethany." The witnesses read the deliberate approach to the place of his coming death, and weigh the chronology that opens the last week.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the providence. Alcuin: "as the time approached in which our Lord had resolved to suffer, he approached the place which he had chosen for the scene of his suffering." Augustine and Chrysostom note that Mary "gave all her attention to our Lord, treating him not as mere man, but as God," and that his later word — "me ye have not always" (v.8) — spoke of his bodily presence, since in majesty he is always with his own (Matthew 28:20).

The grammatical-historical readers fix the date. Robertson notes the transposed pro idiom ("six days before the passover"), paralleled in Amos 1:1, placing the supper on our Saturday evening; Ellicott reserves the full chronology to a separate note, the reckoning depending on the day of the crucifixion. Barnes marks that from Bethany, near Jerusalem, Christ made his triumphal entry (Matthew 21:1). Wesley dates the arrival on the Sabbath the Jews called "the Great Sabbath," Jesus coming from Ephraim (John 11:54).

The Reformed voice draws a lesson on timing. Calvin: those judged "too rashly who thought that Christ would not come to the feast" (John 11:56); we ought "to wait patiently and quietly till the season arrive, which is unknown to us." The Puritan Henry frames it as "the kind visit our Lord Jesus paid to his friends at Bethany." (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown in the merged sources here concerns the Emmaus road and does not bear on this verse.) So the witnesses agree: Christ, in full freedom and on a fixed divine schedule, walks toward his Passion, pausing first among the friends whose love will anoint him for it (v.3).

So the witnesses set the chapter's frame: with the Passion six days off, the Lord turns aside to the household his power had comforted, and the supper of v.2 becomes the threshold of the last week — devotion and treachery, faith and plotting, all gathering around the One who walks knowingly toward the cross.
2:
"There they made him a supper; and Martha served: but Lazarus was one of them that sat at the table with him." The witnesses dwell on the scene — the grateful household, the risen Lazarus at the board — and on harmonizing John with the Synoptics.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the parallels and the chronology. Ellicott and Robertson identify this with the supper of Matthew 26:6 and Mark 14:3 "at the house of Simon the leper," Robertson placing it "two days before the passover" by Mark's reckoning while John "mentions it" earlier; the details "peculiar to St. John, who was an eyewitness," are noted. Barnes observes that Martha and Lazarus are named "because it was not in their own house, but in that of Simon," Lazarus mentioned because it was "so remarkable" that one once dead should be "enjoying again" the fellowship of the table.

The Reformed voice (Calvin) carefully reconciles the accounts: John "does not mention the house, but shows plainly enough that it was in some other place than the house of Lazarus," for he says Lazarus was "one who had been invited along with Christ" — "nor does it involve any contradiction." Gill, however, distinguishes this supper from Simon's as four days later, a minority harmonization.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the social standing: "Martha was a person of some figure, from the great respect paid to her and her sister... as well as from the costly ointment," and "probably it was at their house our Lord and his disciples lodged" each evening of the last week. Lightfoot fixes the day by counting back from the Passover. So the witnesses agree: a real supper of thanksgiving, with the living proof of Christ's power seated at the table — the setting for Mary's act of devotion (v.3).

The witnesses thus read the scene as one of grateful fellowship under the shadow of death: Martha serving, Lazarus living proof of Christ's power, the household pouring out its love before the betrayer pours out his complaint (v.4). The harmonizing care of Calvin and the eyewitness detail of John together anchor the supper firmly in the events of the final week.
3:
"Then took Mary a pound of ointment of spikenard, very costly, and anointed the feet of Jesus, and wiped his feet with her hair." The witnesses read the lavish, humble devotion that fills the house with fragrance.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the substance and the detail. Robertson: a litra (twelve ounces) of "oil of nard," the fragrant spike of an East Indian plant, where Matthew and Mark have "an alabaster cruse." Ellicott notes that John alone names Mary and tells us it was a "pound," true to her earlier character at Christ's feet (Luke 10:39-42). Clarke weighs whether this is the same act as Luke's earlier anointing, concluding (with Wesley) it is distinct — "there were two persons who poured ointment on Christ," one near Nain (Luke 7:37), this one at Bethany.

The Reformed voice (Calvin) notes the nature of the ointment — "not a simple liquor... but a compound of many odoriferous substances," hence its great cost. Gill values it at the three hundred pence of Judas's reckoning (v.5).

Lightfoot uses the passage in the old debate whether this Mary was Mary Magdalene, noting a town "Magdala very near Jerusalem." The witnesses do not all settle that question, but they agree on the act's meaning: an outpouring of costly love, the wiping with her hair a mark of deepest humility — a devotion Judas will at once begrudge (v.4) and Christ will defend as done "against the day of my burying" (v.7). It is, across the schools, the picture of worship that counts no cost against the worth of Christ.

The witnesses thus make Mary's act the chapter's first portrait of true devotion, set deliberately against Judas's calculation (v.5): she gives without measure, wipes his feet with her hair in deepest lowliness, and fills the house with a fragrance that, Christ will say, anticipates his burial (v.7). It is worship that reckons nothing too costly for him.

It is, across the schools, the standard of worship by which the chapter measures every other response to Christ (v.7).
4:
"Then saith one of his disciples, Judas Iscariot, Simon's son, which should betray him..." The witnesses read John's pointed identification of the murmurer, and his characteristic care to name the betrayer.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the harmony. Ellicott: Matthew says "the disciples" asked, Mark "certain persons," but "St. John remembers that it was Judas who spoke, and he remembers that his words were characteristic of the man" (v.6). Robertson discusses the name "Iscariot" — "both father and son were called Iscariot" (man of Kerioth), comparing 6:71 and 13:1.

The Reformed voice reads the synecdoche and the source. Calvin: the murmuring Matthew "attributes to the disciples indiscriminately" and Mark "to some of them," but "it is probable that the murmuring proceeded from Judas alone, and that the rest were induced" to join — "it is customary in Scripture to apply to many, by way of synecdoche, what belongs to one." Gill simply marks the man: "one of his disciples, who had no true love for his master, was an hypocrite, and a covetous person."

So the witnesses agree: the complaint that follows comes, at root, from Judas, whom John names with the standing epithet "which should betray him" — the Evangelist's habit whenever he speaks of him. The objection wears the dress of charity toward the poor, but its source is a heart already turning to treachery, as the next verse will expose (v.6). The contrast with Mary's open-handed love could hardly be sharper: where she pours out, he begrudges; where she worships, he calculates.

The witnesses thus turn the verse into a study in contrast: Mary's lavish love and Judas's grudging arithmetic stand side by side, and John's standing epithet — 'which should betray him' — marks the man whose objection, however pious in sound, springs from a heart already turning to sell his Lord (v.6).

John's habit of naming Judas thus, wherever he appears, keeps the betrayal always before the reader's eye (John 6:71).

Where Mary pours out without measure, Judas calculates and begrudges — the two hearts the supper sets side by side (v.3).
5:
"Why was not this ointment sold for three hundred pence, and given to the poor?" The witnesses read Judas's complaint — a plausible pretext masking covetousness — and weigh the sum.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the value. Robertson: "three hundred pence" (denarii), the genitive of price, matching Mark while Matthew says simply "for much"; all three add "given to the poor." Clarke reckons it at "about £9 13s. 9d. of our money," a labourer's near-yearly wage. Ellicott notes the earlier Gospels preface the figure with the charge of "waste."

The Reformed voice tests the estimate. Calvin cites Pliny: ordinary ointment cost ten denarii a pound, but "the highest price of the best ointment was three hundred and ten denarii"; since the Evangelists agree this was "the most costly," "Judas is correct in valuing a pound at" that sum — the figure is honest even if the heart is not. Gill gives the same conversion into English money.

So the witnesses agree: the objection is shrewd and even accurate in its arithmetic, which is precisely what makes it dangerous — a true fact deployed by a false heart. The appeal to the poor sounds pious, and the other disciples were swept along by it (v.4); but the Evangelist will at once strip away the disguise and show the real motive beneath the charity (v.6). It stands, across the schools, as a warning that the language of benevolence can cloak the love of money, and that the value set on Christ's worship is the truer test of the heart.

The witnesses thus warn that the language of charity can mask the love of money: Judas's figure is accurate, his concern feigned, and the other disciples are swept along (Matthew 26:8). The true measure of the heart, the chapter suggests, is the value it sets on Christ — and the next verse strips away the disguise entirely (v.6).

The accuracy of the figure only sharpens the warning, for a true fact in a false mouth is the most plausible of disguises (v.6).

The appeal to the poor sounds pious, and the disciples are swept along, but the Evangelist will at once lay bare the heart beneath it.
6:
"This he said, not that he cared for the poor; but because he was a thief, and had the bag, and bare what was put therein." The witnesses read John's blunt unmasking of Judas, and debate the force of the last verb.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the role and the verb. Barnes: "the bag" (literally a small case for musicians' reeds) became a purse; Judas, as treasurer of the common fund, kept it. Ellicott: he was "treasurer of the common fund which supplied the wants of the little band, and from which gifts to the poor were made" (John 13:29). Robertson notes John's explanatory aside ("not because he cared for the poor"), adding "clearly the disciples did not know then" of his theft.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke presses the stronger reading of the verb: not merely "bare" but "stole what was put in it" — ebastazen in the sense of pilfering — "the evangelist studies to show the most determined detestation of his conduct," with emphasis on the "he."

The Reformed voice draws the moral. Calvin: "the rest of the Apostles, not from any bad disposition, but thoughtlessly, condemn Mary; but Judas resorts to a plausible pretext for his wickedness," caring "nothing" for the poor — "we are taught what a frightful beast the desire of possessing is." Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic word for the corban-box. So the witnesses agree: the charity was a mask, the man a thief, and his loss of the price of the ointment the goad that will soon drive him to sell his Master (v.4) — covetousness laid bare as the seed of betrayal.

So the witnesses read the verse as John's unflinching exposure: the plea for the poor was a thief's cover, and the lost price of the ointment becomes the goad that drives Judas to the chief priests (Matthew 26:14-16). Covetousness, indulged, is shown as the seed from which the betrayal grows.

The disciples, John notes, did not yet know this of Judas, so quietly had covetousness done its work within the inner circle.

Covetousness indulged in secret is shown, across the schools, as the very seed from which the betrayal of the Master would grow.
7:
"Then said Jesus, Let her alone: against the day of my burying hath she kept this." The witnesses read Christ's defense of Mary — and a textual question that shapes its sense.

The grammatical-historical readers prefer the better-attested reading. Robertson, Ellicott, and Clarke: the strong manuscripts give "suffer her to keep it against the day of my burying" (a purpose clause), rather than the Received Text's "hath she kept this"; the meaning is that she may keep the rest, or that the act itself looks toward his burial (Matthew 26:12; Mark 14:8). Either way the deed is read as an unconscious anointing for death.

The Reformed voice draws the principle. Calvin: in bidding them "let her alone," Christ "shows that they act improperly who disturb their neighbours without a good reason, and raise a disturbance about nothing"; the expense "Judas finds fault with is defended on this ground, that it will serve for his burial." Gill: "do not disturb her... or blame her for it."

Lightfoot uses the verse in the Mary-Magdalene debate, since she is named among those who anointed Christ for burial. (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown in the merged sources here only remarks on the earnestness of the disciples' discussion.) So the witnesses agree: Christ vindicates the despised act as something far greater than waste — a prophetic preparation for his burial, done in love when the calculating could see only cost. Mary, perhaps without fully knowing it, had done for his body what the spices of John 19:40 would later do, and Christ will not have her love rebuked (v.8).

So the witnesses read Christ's defence as a reversal of the verdict: what the calculating called waste, he calls preparation for his burial, and what they would rebuke, he honours. Mary, perhaps unaware, had done for his living body what the spices would later do for his dead (John 19:40), and her love is vindicated against every cold appraisal.

Christ thus turns a rebuke meant for Mary into her highest praise, reading her love as prophecy (Mark 14:8).
8:
"For the poor always ye have with you; but me ye have not always." The witnesses read Christ's distinction between the perpetual duty of charity and the unrepeatable occasion of honouring him in the flesh.

The Eastern father reads the gentleness. Chrysostom hears Christ saying, in effect, "I am a burden, a trouble to you; but wait a little, and I shall be gone" — Augustine adding that this speaks "of his bodily presence," since in his majesty he abides always (Matthew 28:20).

The grammatical-historical readers guard against misuse. Robertson: "Jesus does not discredit gifts to the poor at all. But there is relativity in one's duties" — Mary perceived "with her delicate woman's intuition" what the apostles missed. Barnes cross-refers the abiding duty to Deuteronomy 15:11 and the brief presence to John 16:5-7.

The Reformed voice draws the sharp lesson. Calvin: "a distinction is here drawn between the extraordinary action of Mary, and the daily service which is due to Christ." Those are "apes, and not imitators, who are desirous to serve Christ by costly and splendid display, as if Christ approved of what was done once" rather than commanding ongoing care for the poor — for Christ "did not" intend to sanction perpetual lavish display. Gill reads it as "a sort of prophecy, that there would be always poor persons in the churches of Christ, to be taken care of." So the witnesses agree: charity to the poor is the standing, daily duty; Mary's act was the seizing of a unique moment that would never return — and both, rightly understood, honour Christ.

So the witnesses guard the verse from misuse: it neither discounts the poor — a perpetual charge (Deut 15:11) — nor sanctions lavish display as the norm, but marks the unrepeatable moment when Christ in the flesh could be honoured as Mary honoured him. Both the daily duty and the singular act, rightly seen, are love to him.

Rightly heard, then, the saying neither chills charity nor licenses display, but honours the One whose brief bodily presence Mary so well discerned.
9:
"Much people of the Jews therefore knew that he was there: and they came not for Jesus' sake only, but that they might see Lazarus also." The witnesses read the crowds drawn by the great miracle, and its providential effect.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the detail. Robertson notes the idiom "the common people" (ho ochlos polus, "the people much"). Ellicott: pilgrims from Jericho would have spread the news in Jerusalem, where the city was "earnestly asking about him" (John 11:55); they go to Bethany "in large numbers," and this leads the writer to add that many "believed." Clarke notes John, a Galilean, often calls the Jerusalemites "the Jews."

The Reformed voice reads the providence. Calvin: "the more nearly the time of the death of Christ approached, it became the more necessary that his name should be universally celebrated, in order that it might be a preparation for stronger faith after his death" — "the recent miracle of the resurrection of Lazarus had acquired great celebrity." Gill: Bethany being "but two miles from Jerusalem, the report... soon reached thither."

So the witnesses agree: the raising of Lazarus draws the crowds doubly — to see both the Raiser and the raised — and God uses the resulting fame to spread Christ's name on the eve of his death. The living Lazarus is himself a standing witness, which is precisely why the rulers will plot to silence him too (v.10). The very miracle that should have led the nation to faith becomes, for the leaders, one more reason to kill — the irony the chapter will press to its tragic height (v.37).

So the witnesses read the gathering crowds as the providential fame God spreads on the eve of the cross: the risen Lazarus draws men doubly, to see both Raiser and raised, and his living testimony is precisely why the rulers now plot to silence him too (v.10). The miracle that should have led to faith hardens the leaders to murder.

The living Lazarus thus becomes a sign the rulers can neither deny nor abide, and their answer is not faith but the plot to kill (v.10).
10:
"But the chief priests consulted that they might put Lazarus also to death." The witnesses read the murderous logic of unbelief — to destroy the living evidence it cannot deny.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the actors. Robertson: the Sanhedrin had already resolved to kill Jesus (John 11:53) and sought information to arrest him (John 11:57); now "the Sadducees were specially active." Ellicott notes the chief priests were "for the most part Sadducees" (Acts 5:17), and Lazarus's life "is a witness to the divinity of him whom they have condemned, and a denial of their own doctrine" that there is no resurrection.

The Wesleyan-Arminian and grammatical-historical voices mark the blindness. Barnes: "when men are determined not to believe the gospel, there is no end to the crimes to which they are driven" — the resurrection was "so clear that they could not resist it," so they would destroy the proof. Clarke: "how blind not to perceive that he who had raised him" could raise him again.

The Reformed voice names the madness. Calvin: "it certainly was worse than insane fury to endeavour to put to death one who had manifestly been raised from the dead by divine power" — "such is the spirit of giddiness with which Satan torments the wicked." Wesley adds a historical note: "here is the plain reason why the other evangelists, who wrote while Lazarus was living, did not relate his story." So the witnesses agree: faced with a miracle they could neither deny nor explain, the rulers chose to bury the evidence — unbelief hardening into the will to kill both the Raiser and the raised (v.11).

So the witnesses read the plot against Lazarus as unbelief's last logic: unable to deny the miracle, the rulers would destroy its evidence. Calvin's 'worse than insane fury' and Barnes's 'no end to the crimes' together expose a hardness that meets divine power not with faith but with the will to kill both the sign and the One who gave it (v.11).

Wesley adds the telling note that this is why the earlier Gospels, written while Lazarus lived, pass his story over in silence.
11:
"Because that by reason of him many of the Jews went away, and believed on Jesus." A brief verse, and the witnesses read the spreading secession from the rulers' authority to faith in Christ.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the tenses. Ellicott renders it "were going away, and believing on Jesus" — "the tenses mark the continuance of the secession," and the two verbs "mark the two acts": withdrawing "from the authority of the priests" and believing on Jesus (v.18). Robertson: the inchoative imperfect hypēgon, "began to withdraw," as happened at the raising of Lazarus (John 11:45), "and the secession was still going on."

The verse, on every reading, supplies the rulers' motive in v.10: it was not the bare fact of the miracle but its effect — a steady defection of the people to Christ — that they could not tolerate. Gill: "by reason of" Lazarus, "of the miracle wrought upon him," of which he was "a living and an abiding witness."

So the witnesses agree: Lazarus's restored life was not a private mercy only but a public testimony that drew many from the priests to the Messiah, and it is precisely this drawing-power that the rulers move to stop — by killing the witness. The chapter thus sets the deepening faith of the people against the hardening murder of the leaders, the two responses that the triumphal entry will display side by side (v.19), and that the Evangelist will finally trace to the prophesied unbelief of the nation (v.37).

So the witnesses read the verse as the rulers' true motive laid bare: not the bare miracle but its effect — a steady defection of the people to Christ — is what they cannot bear. Lazarus's restored life is a public summons, and the chapter sets the people's deepening faith against the leaders' deepening murder (v.19).

It is the drawing-power of the miracle, not the miracle alone, that the rulers move to stop — by silencing the witness himself.

The chapter thus sets the people's deepening faith against the leaders' deepening murder, the two responses the entry will soon display together.
12:
"On the next day much people that were come to the feast... took branches of palm trees, and went forth to meet him." The witnesses read the triumphal entry — John's brief account of an event all four Gospels record.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the day and crowd. Robertson: "on the morrow," our Palm Sunday; "a great multitude" (the same idiom as "the common people" of v.9). Ellicott notes John tells "the outer incidents... more briefly than any one of the others" (Matthew 21:1-11; Mark 11:1-11; Luke 19:29-44). Wesley: the multitude "consisted chiefly of Galileans, not men of Jerusalem."

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the providence and the typology. Chrysostom recalls the Passover lamb "shut up" on the tenth day and kept to the fourteenth (Exodus 12:3-6) — the true Lamb now entering. Theophylact warns that when they called him "King of Israel" they "dreamed of an earthly king" who would "deliver them from the Romans." Augustine reads the crowds drawn by "that sweet savour" of the Lazarus miracle.

The Reformed and Puritan voices read the design. Calvin: "Christ's design, which was that he came to Jerusalem of his own accord, to offer himself to die; for it was necessary that his death should be voluntary." Henry marks "the respect that was paid to our Lord Jesus by the common people." So the witnesses agree: the King comes — but in meekness, on his own deliberate terms — and the very acclaim that hails him as deliverer misreads the kingdom he brings, the misunderstanding the next verses will set against the prophecy he fulfils (v.15).

So the witnesses read the triumphal entry as the meeting of true acclaim and mistaken hope: the King comes, but in the meekness his own prophecy foretold (v.15), and the crowd's cry of 'King of Israel' both honours him and misreads the kingdom he brings — a deliverance not from Rome but from sin and death.

The King comes deliberately, Calvin notes, to offer himself to a death that must be wholly voluntary if it is to redeem (John 10:18).
13:
"...Hosanna: Blessed is the King of Israel that cometh in the name of the Lord." The witnesses read the people's acclamation — the palms of victory and the cry of Psalm 118:25-26 — and the misunderstanding within it.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the detail. Robertson: "the branches of the palm-trees" (baia tōn phoinikōn), an Egyptian-origin word found also in Revelation 7:9 and 1 Maccabees. Ellicott: "the palm branches of the palm trees," growing on the spot. Barnes and Wesley ground the cry in Psalm 118:26; Lightfoot supplies rabbinic lore on the festal palm.

The Reformed voice reads the symbolism. Calvin: the palm "was the emblem of victory and peace among the ancients," used when "they bestowed kingly power on any one"; the people took them "as a token of gladness... at receiving a new king," testifying that "they acknowledged" Jesus as the promised one. Gill: the palm, "a sign of joy and victory," grew plentifully near Bethany ("house of dates").

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem ties "King of Israel" to the oath to David of an everlasting throne (Psalm 132:11). (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown here is off-topic.) So the witnesses agree: the crowd rightly hails Jesus as the coming King in the Lord's name, fulfilling the Psalm — yet, as Theophylact warned (v.12), they dream of an earthly deliverer; the acclamation is true in its words and mistaken in its hope, the gap the prophecy of the lowly King on the colt will expose (v.15).

So the witnesses read the palms and the Hosanna as a true confession spoken in partial blindness: the words of Psalm 118:26 rightly hail the coming King in the Lord's name, yet the hope behind them dreams of an earthly throne. The lowly colt of the next verse will quietly correct the cry (v.14).

Cyril ties the title to the Davidic oath of an everlasting throne, so that the crowd confesses more than it understands (Ps 132:11).

The lowly colt of the next verse will quietly correct the crowd's dream of an earthly deliverer (v.14).
14:
"And Jesus, when he had found a young ass, sat thereon." A brief verse, and the witnesses read John's spare notice, which exists chiefly to introduce the prophecy of v.15.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the detail and the harmony. Robertson: onarion, "a young ass," a late diminutive found only here in the New Testament; the finding was "through the disciples, of course" (Mark 11:2-6; Matthew 21:2-3; Luke 19:30). Ellicott: "St. John simply mentions this to introduce the prophecy," and "having found" does not exclude the search the other Evangelists record (cf. John 9:35).

The Reformed voice notes John's method. Calvin: "this part of the history is more minutely related by the other Evangelists," who tell how Christ "sent for an ass" (Matthew 21:1); "John, who was the latest writer of all the Evangelists, reckoned it enough to notice briefly the substance," leaving out many circumstances. Gill: "which he sent his disciples for, to a neighbouring village."

So the witnesses agree: the verse is deliberately compressed, John assuming his readers know the fuller Synoptic account and recording only what he needs to anchor the prophecy. The choice of the ass — the mount of peace, not the war-horse — is itself the point, and the next verse will name the Scripture it fulfils, so that the King's lowly manner of entry interprets the crowd's royal acclaim (v.13). The Evangelist's brevity is thus not indifference but the confidence that the prophecy he is about to quote will say what the bare fact cannot.

So the witnesses read John's spare notice as deliberate: he records only what anchors the prophecy, trusting the fuller Synoptic account, and lets the choice of the ass — the mount of peace, not war — speak for itself. The bare fact waits for the Scripture of v.15 to disclose its meaning.

The Evangelist's confidence is that the prophecy he is about to quote will say what the bare fact of the colt cannot (v.15).

John's brevity is not indifference but the confidence that the Scripture he quotes will carry the weight the bare fact cannot.
15:
"Fear not, daughter of Sion: behold, thy King cometh, sitting on an ass's colt." The witnesses read the prophecy of Zechariah 9:9 that interprets the entry — the King who comes in meekness and peace.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the citation. Robertson: "the quotation is from Zechariah 9:9 shortened"; "the ass was the animal ridden in peace as the horse was in war" (Judges 10:4; 2 Samuel 17:23) — "Zechariah pictures one coming in peace." Ellicott notes the free, abbreviated form (fuller in Matthew 21:5), and that John's rendering "is nearer to the Hebrew" than the Septuagint.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the tone: "Fear not — for his meekness forbids fear, as well as the end of his coming." Barnes simply anchors it in Zechariah 9:9. Gill points back to the prophecy with joy: "but rejoice."

So the witnesses agree: the manner of the entry is the message. The acclaiming crowd dreamed of a conquering king (v.13), but the prophecy hails a King "meek, and sitting upon an ass" — one who comes in peace, not in arms, to die rather than to drive out Rome. The colt corrects the cry: this King's victory will be won on a cross, not a battlefield. The disciples, the next verse will note, grasped none of this at the time, understanding only after Jesus was glorified that the entry had fulfilled the ancient word (v.16). The fulfilment thus stands as a quiet rebuke to every earthly hope laid on the Messiah.

So the witnesses read the manner of the entry as its message: the prophecy of Zechariah 9:9 hails a King meek and peaceable, whose victory will be won on a cross, not a battlefield. The colt corrects the crowd's dream, and the disciples, the next verse notes, grasp none of it until Christ is glorified (v.16).

The fulfilment thus stands as a quiet rebuke to every earthly hope laid upon the Messiah, who comes to die rather than to conquer Rome.

This King's victory, the prophecy implies, will be won upon a cross and not a battlefield, in peace and not in arms.
16:
"These things understood not his disciples at the first: but when Jesus was glorified, then remembered they... that they had done these things unto him." The witnesses read John's candid note on the disciples' delayed understanding.

The grammatical-historical readers mark the Johannine touch. Robertson: "another comment by John concerning the failure of the disciples to know what was happening" (John 2:22; John 7:39); "was glorified" (edoxasthē) covers "his death, resurrection, and ascension." Ellicott: "a touch peculiar to St. John, and exactly in his manner" — he "remembers the difference between the spiritual receptivity before and after Pentecost," how "the Old Testament Scriptures became filled with a new life." Clarke: "it is only in the light of the new covenant that the old is to be fully understood."

The Reformed voice reads the providence. Calvin: "as the seed does not spring up as soon as it is thrown into the earth, so the result of the works of God is not immediately seen"; the Apostles "are the servants of God to fulfil the prophecy, but they do not understand what they are doing."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley draws the lesson of faith: "the design of God's providential dispensations is seldom understood at first. We ought therefore to believe, though we understand not, and to give ourselves up to the Divine disposal" — "the great work of faith is to embrace those things which we know not now, but shall know hereafter." So the witnesses agree: the disciples acted in the very fulfilment of prophecy without grasping it, and only the glorifying of Christ and the gift of the Spirit unlocked the meaning — a pattern of how the Scriptures open only in the light of the risen Lord.

So the witnesses read the disciples' delayed understanding as a pattern of faith: they fulfilled prophecy unawares, and only the glorifying of Christ and the gift of the Spirit unlocked the meaning (John 7:39). The Scriptures, Wesley's lesson runs, open only in the light of the risen Lord, so we believe before we fully understand.

Only the risen and glorified Christ, and the Spirit he sends, unlock the Scriptures the disciples had lived without grasping (Luke 24:45).
17:
"The people therefore that was with him when he called Lazarus out of his grave... bare record." The witnesses read the testifying crowd — the eyewitnesses of the raising who spread the report and swelled the acclaim.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the two crowds and the text. Robertson: this "crowning triumph" gave "an added sense of importance to the crowds that were actually with Jesus when he called Lazarus" (John 11:45); the imperfect emarturei, "bare witness," of a continuing testimony. Ellicott notes a textual variant ("bare record that he called Lazarus") turning only on one letter, and that this crowd of eyewitnesses is distinct from the festal multitude.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley admires the Evangelist's restraint: "how admirably does the apostle express both the greatness of the miracle and the facility with which it was wrought! The easiness of the Scripture style on the most grand occurrences is more sublime than all the pomp of orators."

The Reformed voice reads the cause of the acclaim. Calvin: "many persons, aroused by the report of so great a miracle, came to meet Christ," the rumour "widely spread," and "they had good reason for ascribing to the son of Mary the honour of the Messiah." Cyril of Jerusalem joins the witness to Christ's exaltation. So the witnesses agree: the triumphal entry was no spontaneous burst but the fruit of testimony — the eyewitnesses of Lazarus's raising bearing record, the report drawing the multitude, and the living miracle becoming the engine of the day's honour to Christ, as the next verse states plainly (v.18).

So the witnesses read the testifying crowd as the engine of the day's honour: the eyewitnesses of Lazarus's raising bore continuing record, the report drew the multitude, and the living miracle turned rumour into a royal welcome. The acclaim of the entry rests, the next verse says plainly, on that one supreme sign (v.18).

The triumphal entry was thus no spontaneous burst but the fruit of testimony borne to the greatest of the signs.

The living miracle thus became the engine of the day's honour, as the next verse states in plain words (v.18).
18:
"For this cause the people also met him, for that they heard that he had done this miracle." A brief verse, and the witnesses read the raising of Lazarus as the specific cause of the multitude's welcome.

The grammatical-historical readers distinguish the crowds and weigh the emphasis. Robertson: "the multitude" of v.13, "not the crowd just mentioned that had been with Jesus" at the raising — "there were two crowds (one following Jesus, one meeting Jesus)"; the verb hypēntēsen, "went and met him." Ellicott: "the emphatic form of the sentence points out that the raising of Lazarus was the miracle which carried the entire conviction of the multitude" — they had heard of other miracles, "but this stood by itself."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley draws the two streams together: "the multitude went to meet him, because they heard" from the eyewitnesses; "so in a little time both joined together, to go before and to follow him." Gill: this was "a principal reason... which induced them" to greet him as King of Israel.

So the witnesses agree: John, having noted the eyewitness testimony (v.17), now names its effect — the raising of Lazarus was the decisive sign that turned report into a royal welcome. The two crowds, the followers and the greeters, converge around the King on the colt, both moved by the one supreme miracle. The day's acclaim thus rests squarely on Lazarus's restored life — the very witness the rulers had resolved to extinguish (v.10), and which their fear will lament in the next verse (v.19).

So the witnesses read the convergence of the two crowds — followers and greeters — around the King on the colt, both moved by the raising of Lazarus, the sign that 'carried the entire conviction.' The day's honour thus rests on the very witness the rulers had resolved to extinguish (v.10), and which their despair will lament (v.19).

The two crowds, the followers and the greeters, meet around the King on the colt, both drawn by the one supreme miracle.

The day's acclaim rests squarely on the very witness the rulers had resolved to extinguish (v.10).
19:
"The Pharisees therefore said among themselves, Perceive ye how ye prevail nothing? behold, the world is gone after him." The witnesses read the leaders' despairing self-reproach — and an unwitting prophecy.

The grammatical-historical readers picture the scene. Robertson: "a graphic picture of the predicament of the Pharisees standing off and watching the enthusiastic crowds sweep by"; "as people usually do, they blame each other for the defeat of their plots." Ellicott prefers the indicative — "ye perceive how ye profit nothing" — "they blame each other for the failure of all their plans" (John 11:47) "and prepare to accept the counsel of Caiaphas." Barnes: their failure "induced them to hasten their design of putting him to death" (John 11:53).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke fixes the idiom: "the world is gone after him" answers to "the French tout le monde" — "the bulk of the people"; Lightfoot notes the Talmudists' like phrase, "all the world is gone after him."

The Reformed voice reads the rage beneath the despair. Calvin: "by these words they urge themselves to greater rage," reproaching their own "slothfulness and cowardice" — "this is the way desperate men talk when making themselves ready for any extreme attempt." So the witnesses agree: in their frustration the Pharisees blurt out a truth larger than they know — "the world is gone after him" — an unconscious foreshadowing of the Gentiles who, in the very next scene, come seeking Jesus (v.20). Their lament at his spreading success becomes, like Caiaphas's word (John 11:51), a prophecy against themselves.

So the witnesses read the Pharisees' lament as an unwitting prophecy: in their frustration they blurt a truth larger than they know — 'the world is gone after him' — and in the very next scene the world arrives, as Gentiles come seeking Jesus (v.20). Like Caiaphas (John 11:51), they prophesy against themselves.

Their lament at his spreading success becomes, like the high priest's word, a prophecy fulfilled against themselves (John 11:51).

In their frustration the Pharisees blurt a truth far larger than they know, and the world arrives in the very next scene (v.20).
20:
"And there were certain Greeks among them that came up to worship at the feast." The witnesses read the arrival of the Greeks as a firstfruits of the Gentiles — the "world" of v.19 beginning to come.

The grammatical-historical readers fix their identity. Robertson: "real Greeks, not Greek-speaking Jews (Hellenists)," but Gentiles like those of Antioch (Acts 11:20) — "probably proselytes of the gate or God-fearers" (Acts 17:4). Ellicott: "not Hellenists... but Hellenes," who "were in the habit of going up to Jerusalem at the feasts" (John 7:35). Barnes lists the three views; Clarke the same. Bede recalls that "many Gentiles from distant countries came to worship," as the Ethiopian eunuch (Acts 8:27).

The Reformed voice debates their status. Calvin doubts they were uncircumcised, since they "came to worship"; he takes them as Jews of the dispersion, given Roman laws against conversion. But most read them as Gentile worshippers.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the sign: "certain Greeks — a prelude of the Gentile Church... they came up on purpose to worship the God of Israel." Henry: "honour is here paid to Christ by certain Greeks that enquired for him with respect." So the witnesses agree (Calvin dissenting on their precise status): just as the Pharisees lament that "the world is gone after him," the world arrives in person — Gentiles seeking Jesus — and their coming will draw from Christ the announcement that his hour, and the harvest of the nations, has come (v.23).

So the witnesses read the coming of the Greeks as the firstfruits of the nations: just as the rulers lament that the world has gone after him, the world arrives in person, and their seeking will draw from Christ the announcement that his hour — and the harvest of the Gentiles — has come (v.23).

Their coming, the firstfruits of the nations, fulfils in person the Pharisees' despairing cry that the world has gone after him (v.19).

Their seeking will draw from Christ the announcement that his hour, and the harvest of the nations, has now come (v.23).
21:
"The same came therefore to Philip, which was of Bethsaida of Galilee, and desired him, saying, Sir, we would see Jesus." The witnesses read the Greeks' modest, reverent approach through a disciple with a Greek name.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the connection. Robertson: Philip "had a Greek name and the Greeks may have seen Philip in Galilee where there were many Greeks," probably the western Bethsaida (Mark 6:45); the imperfect ērōtōn, "began to ask." Barnes: "would see Jesus" implies "a desire to converse with him," prompted by curiosity or "a desire to be instructed." Clarke suggests they were "of Phoenicia or Syria," addressing Philip as "probably known to them."

The Reformed voice reads the reverence. Calvin: "it is an indication of reverence that they do not address Christ, but are desirous to obtain access through Philip; for reverence always begets modesty" — and he turns sharply on the Papist inference "that we ought to call on departed saints" as advocates, calling it "so ridiculous that it does not deserve a reply."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the courtesy: "they spake to him as to one they were little acquainted with. We would see Jesus — a modest request." So the witnesses agree: the Gentiles come seeking, not demanding, approaching through a disciple in becoming humility — their simple wish, "we would see Jesus," standing as the perennial cry of the seeking heart, and the occasion that moves Christ to speak of the cross by which all nations would be drawn to him (v.32).

So the witnesses read 'we would see Jesus' as the perennial cry of the seeking heart, spoken in reverent humility through a disciple. The Gentiles come not demanding but asking, and their simple wish becomes the occasion of Christ's word about the cross by which all nations would be drawn to him (v.32).

Their reverent approach through a disciple stands, across the schools, as the model of the heart that comes to Christ seeking, not demanding.

Their simple wish, 'we would see Jesus,' stands as the perennial cry of every heart that comes to him in earnest.
22:
"Philip cometh and telleth Andrew: and again Andrew and Philip tell Jesus." The witnesses read the disciples' careful, united handling of an unexpected request.

The grammatical-historical readers note the names and the drama. Robertson marks "the vivid dramatic presents — cometh, telleth"; Andrew, "a man of wisdom for a crisis," who first brought Simon to Jesus (John 1:41). Ellicott calls it "a striking coincidence, and perhaps more than this, that the Greeks thus came into connection with the only Apostles who bear Greek names" — Philip and Andrew, paired before (John 1:44; John 6:7-8). Barnes wonders why Philip did not go straight to Jesus: "possibly he was doubtful whether Jesus would wish to converse with Gentiles."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the lesson of fellowship: "how pleasing to God is this union, when the ministers of his Gospel agree and unite together to bring souls to Christ. But where self-love prevails... this union never exists. Bigotry often ruins every generous sentiment."

The Reformed voice (Gill) reads Philip's prudence — consulting Andrew, "a senior man" of the same town, before bringing the matter forward. So the witnesses agree: the request of the Greeks passes through a chain of humble, cooperative discipleship — Philip to Andrew, both to Jesus — a small picture of how the nations are brought to Christ not by one self-important mediator but by disciples working together. The hesitation over whether Jesus would receive Gentiles makes his answer the more striking, for it will declare that his death is precisely for the world (v.23).

So the witnesses read the careful chain — Philip to Andrew, both to Jesus — as a small picture of how the nations are brought to Christ: not by one self-important mediator but by humble, cooperative disciples. The hesitation over whether Jesus would receive Gentiles makes his answer the more striking, for it declares his death is for the world (v.23).

That the request passes through the two apostles with Greek names is, Ellicott suggests, more than mere coincidence (John 1:44).

The nations, the scene quietly teaches, are brought to Christ not by one proud mediator but by humble disciples working together.
23:
"And Jesus answered them, saying, The hour is come, that the Son of man should be glorified." The witnesses read the Greeks' coming as the trigger for Christ's announcement that his appointed hour has arrived.

The grammatical-historical readers fix "the hour." Robertson: "the predestined hour, seen from the start" (John 2:4), not yet come at 7:30 and 8:20, now come (John 13:1; John 17:1) — "the request from the Greeks for this interview stirs the heart of Jesus." Ellicott: the words "are rather the utterance of the thoughts of his own mind, which this visit suggests, than an answer."

The witnesses divide over what "glorified" names. The Wesleyan-Arminian and most read it of his death-and-exaltation. Clarke: "the time is just at hand in which the Gospel shall be preached to all nations, the middle wall broken down" — "but this could not be till after his death and resurrection." Wesley: "glorified with the Father and in the sight of every creature. But he must suffer first."

The Reformed voice gives the other emphasis. Calvin: "many explain this as referring to the death of Christ... but I rather view it as referring to the publication of the gospel" — "the knowledge of him would soon be spread through every region of the world." Cyril of Jerusalem notes he "came to his passion willingly." So the witnesses agree the hour is the hinge of all — Calvin stressing the worldwide harvest the Greeks foreshadow, the others the cross by which it comes — and both meet in the truth that the glorifying of the Son of man passes through death to the gathering of the nations (v.24).

So the witnesses read 'the hour is come' as the chapter's hinge: the Greeks' arrival signals that the time of glorifying the Son of man has arrived — whether read of his death (most) or the worldwide harvest it brings (Calvin), the two meeting in the truth that glory comes through the cross (v.24).

Both readings of 'glorified' meet in the cross, by which the Son of man is exalted and the nations gathered (v.24).
24:
"Except a corn of wheat fall into the ground and die, it abideth alone: but if it die, it bringeth forth much fruit." The witnesses read the great parable of fruitful death — Christ's own, and his disciples'.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers fix the figure. Robertson: "the grain of wheat" must fall and die, else it remains "by itself alone." Clarke: "our Lord compares himself to a grain of wheat; his death to a grain sown and decomposed; his resurrection to the blade which springs up" — "I must die to be glorified." Barnes admires "the wisdom by which he introduces the subject of his death": they had seen his triumph and looked for a kingdom; he tells them he is to be glorified — but through death. Lightfoot ties it to the Greeks: "the time draws on wherein I must be glorified in the conversion of the Gentiles."

The Reformed voice presses the paradox. Calvin: Christ "compares his death to sowing, which appears to tend to the destruction of the wheat, but yet is the cause of far more abundant increase" — an admonition "especially necessary at that time."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley notes the aptness: "the late resurrection of Lazarus gave our Lord a natural occasion of speaking on this subject," and he chose "almost the only" seed "that dies in the earth." Cyril of Jerusalem joins the proofs of eternal life. So the witnesses agree: the death of the Son of man is no defeat but the law of all spiritual harvest — the single grain buried that the world's many might live; and the same law, the next verse insists, governs every disciple who would follow him (v.25).

So the witnesses read the grain of wheat as the law of all spiritual harvest: the single seed buried that the many might live. Christ's death is no defeat but the very condition of fruitfulness, and the same law, the next verse insists, governs every disciple who would follow him (v.25).

The single grain buried that the world's many might live is, across the schools, the law of all spiritual harvest (v.25).
25:
"He that loveth his life shall lose it; and he that hateth his life in this world shall keep it unto life eternal." The witnesses read the law of the grain applied to the disciple — the paradox of saving by losing.

The grammatical-historical readers note the saying's breadth. Robertson: "this great saying was spoken at various times" (Mark 8:35; Matthew 16:25; Luke 17:33); the present "loseth it" is the paradox already at work. Ellicott prefers "he that loveth his life loses it" — the loss "not in the future only, but... in every moment when a man loves and seeks to save his own life." Barnes calls it "a favourite principle, a sort of axiom" of the Lord (Matthew 10:39).

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices fix the comparison. Wesley: "he that loveth his life — more than the will of God — shall lose it eternally; and he that hateth his life — in comparison of the will of God — shall preserve it." Clarke: the disciples should imitate Christ, ready, "when called to it, to lay down their lives for the truth."

The Reformed and Eastern voices read the heart. Calvin: "if we must die in order that we may bring forth fruit, we ought patiently to permit God to mortify us"; the "hatred" is set against "immoderate desire of the present life." Chrysostom: "sweet is the present life... yet not to all, but to those who are riveted to it"; one who looks to heaven "will soon despise this life." So the witnesses agree: the cross is not Christ's alone but the pattern of discipleship — only the life surrendered for God's will is truly kept, and the one who clings to it loses it; which the next verse makes the very condition of serving and following him (v.26).

So the witnesses read the paradox as the pattern of discipleship: the life clutched is lost, the life surrendered for God's will is kept. The cross is not Christ's alone but the shape of every true following, and the next verse makes this the very condition of serving and being honoured by the Father (v.26).
26:
"If any man serve me, let him follow me; and where I am, there shall also my servant be: if any man serve me, him will my Father honour." The witnesses read the law of the grain (v.24) made the condition of discipleship — and crowned with a promise.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the structure. Robertson: "let him keep on following" (present imperative); "where I am, there" — "in presence and spiritually." Ellicott insists the Greeks are still in view: "the point of the whole teaching is missed unless we think of the Greeks as present" — the law of sacrifice applies to "those who follow him," not Christ alone. Barnes: "let him imitate me; do what I do, bear what I bear," the passage having "reference to calamity" and the cross.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke unfolds the double role and reward: Christ is master "to instruct" and "to employ"; his servant becomes "his disciple" and "his servant," with a "twofold promise" — to "be with Christ" in eternity and to be "honoured" by the Father.

The Reformed voice presses the example. Calvin: "that death may not be exceedingly bitter to us, Christ invites us by his example to submit to it cheerfully"; on "no other condition does he admit us into their number, except that we follow the path which he points out" — "he leads the way to suffer death." So the witnesses agree: the Greeks who "would see Jesus" (v.21) are told that to find him is to follow him through death to glory; the servant's place is where the Master is, and the Father himself will honour those who tread the way of the grain that dies to bear fruit.

So the witnesses read the verse as the answer to the Greeks who 'would see Jesus' (v.21): to find him is to follow him through death to glory, the servant's place being where the Master is. The Father himself, Christ promises, will honour those who tread the way of the grain that dies to bear fruit.

The Greeks who would see Jesus thus learn that to find him is to follow him, even through death, to where the Master is.
27:
"Now is my soul troubled; and what shall I say? Father, save me from this hour: but for this cause came I unto this hour." The witnesses read John's "Gethsemane" — the real shrinking of Christ's human soul, and its instant submission.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the humanity. Robertson: the soul (psychē) here is "synonymous with spirit" of John 13:21; "is troubled" (perfect passive, used of Jesus also in John 11:33), "an echo" of Psalm 6:3, Psalm 42:5 — "while John proves the deity of Jesus, he assumes throughout his real humanity." Ellicott: the same word as "life" in v.25, "the seat of the natural feelings," with "a real shrinking from the darkness." Barnes: "his human nature shrunk from the scenes through which he was to pass" (Luke 22:42-44).

The witnesses divide over "save me from this hour." The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads it not as a wish but a labouring for utterance: "not what shall I choose? For his heart was fixed... but he laboured for utterance" — "save me from this hour" and "for this cause came I" glancing at the two sides of his mind. The Reformed Calvin: "by shrinking from death he confesses his cowardice" only in appearance; there is real conflict, yet "nothing" inconsistent with submission.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the comfort for us. Chrysostom: against those who thought it easy for one "out of reach of human pain" to talk of death, Christ shows real feeling. Augustine and Henry note the Father at once honours him with a voice. So the witnesses agree: Christ's trouble is genuine — he sanctifies our dread by feeling it — yet his will never wavers from the hour for which he came, the cross he immediately embraces by asking that the Father's name, not his own ease, be glorified (v.28).

So the witnesses read the troubled soul as Christ's sanctifying of our dread: his shrinking is real, that he might feel our fear, yet his will never wavers from the hour for which he came. From the trouble he rises at once to ask not for his own ease but for the Father's glory (v.28).
28:
"Father, glorify thy name. Then came there a voice from heaven, saying, I have both glorified it, and will glorify it again." The witnesses read Christ's self-forgetting prayer and the Father's audible answer.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the prayer and the voice. Robertson: the aorist imperative carries "a note of urgency"; this is "the prayer of the spirit as opposed to that of the flesh" of v.27, the "name" expressing the character of God. Ellicott: "the Son's will is one with the Father's; the Son's glory is in glorifying the Father's name" (cf. Matthew 6:9). Lightfoot notes the weight of a petition answered "by an audible voice" from heaven.

The Wesleyan-Arminian and grammatical-historical voices read the content. Wesley: "I have glorified it — by thy entrance into this hour; and I will glorify it — by thy passing through it." Barnes paraphrases the prayer: "let thy name be honoured... whatever sufferings it may cost me," so that "God's glory is to be the great end" of all. Clarke: by Christ's death and resurrection the divine attributes "were all more abundantly glorified than they had ever been before."

The Reformed voice reads the surrender. Calvin: "he prefers the glory of God to all things else, and even disregards his own life"; "the true regulation of all our desires is to seek the glory of God in such a manner that all other things shall give way to it." So the witnesses agree: from the trouble of v.27 Christ rises at once to the prayer that sums his whole life — "glorify thy name" — and the Father answers from heaven, pledging that the cross now approaching will glorify him as the past has done; the third such heavenly testimony to the Son (Matthew 3:17; Matthew 17:5).

So the witnesses read the prayer and the voice as the summit of self-surrender: 'glorify thy name' gathers Christ's whole life into one petition, and the Father answers audibly, pledging that the approaching cross will glorify him as the past has done — the third such heavenly testimony to the Son (Matthew 17:5).
29:
"The people therefore... said that it thundered: others said, An angel spake to him." The witnesses read the crowd's divided perception of the heavenly voice — and what it reveals about hardened hearing.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the report. Robertson: "that thunder came to pass," the crowd "standing by," "but Jesus understood his Father's voice." Barnes: the words were spoken distinctly (Matthew 3:17), yet some "from amazement or envy" took it for "a mere natural phenomenon." Clarke (with Pearce): "probably there was thunder as well as a voice, as in Exodus 19:16-17," some at a distance hearing only the thunder. Ellicott marks this as "strong testimony" that the narrative was "written by one who was present."

The Reformed voice reads the dullness. Calvin: "it was truly monstrous that the assembled multitude were unmoved by so evident a miracle. Some are so deaf that they hear as a confused sound what God had distinctly pronounced; others... take away much from the majesty of the Divine voice." The same happens daily, "for God speaks plainly" yet is not heard.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley draws the lesson: "in the most glorious revelations there may remain something obscure, to exercise our faith"; thunder "did frequently attend a voice from heaven." So the witnesses agree: the same voice that confirmed Christ to himself was variously heard — distinctly, as an angel, or as mere thunder — a parable of how the clearest divine testimony lands differently on hearts according to their state; and Christ at once explains that the voice came not for his sake but for theirs (v.30).

So the witnesses read the crowd's divided hearing — thunder, an angel, or a voice — as a parable of how the clearest divine testimony lands differently on hearts according to their state. Calvin's 'monstrous' dullness stands as a warning, and Christ at once explains that the voice came not for his sake but for theirs (v.30).

The same voice that confirmed Christ to himself lands variously on hearts according to their state — distinct word, angel, or mere thunder.
30:
"Jesus answered and said, This voice came not because of me, but for your sakes." The witnesses read Christ's word that the heavenly testimony was a sign for the people, not a support he needed.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the sense. Barnes: "not to strengthen or confirm me... but to give you a striking and indubitable proof that I am the Messiah," to be remembered after his death. Ellicott: "Jesus himself knew that the Father heareth him always, but this answer is a sign to others" (cf. John 11:41-42). Robertson meets the seeming contradiction with vv.28–29: take it as "the figure of exaggerated contrast — not merely for my sake, but also for yours."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke ties it to the Greeks: the voice was "probably meaning those Greeks" newly come, who "had never any such opportunity" as the Jews to see his miracles — "for their sakes" the firstfruits of the Gentiles.

The Reformed voice reads the principle. Calvin: "as it was on our account that Christ clothed himself with flesh, so all the blessings which he received from the Father were bestowed on our account" — he "had no need" of the voice himself. So the witnesses agree: the Father's audible witness, like the incarnation itself, was for the people's faith, not the Son's reassurance — a public seal on Christ's mission given for the sake of believers (and perhaps the seeking Greeks), and a pattern that all Christ received he received for us. From this confirmed mission he turns to declare the world-judging power of his approaching death (v.31).

So the witnesses read the verse as a window on the whole economy of grace: as Christ took flesh for us, so every blessing he received from the Father — even this voice — was for our faith, not his reassurance. The public seal on his mission given, he turns to declare the world-judging power of his death (v.31).

From this confirmed mission Christ turns at once to declare the world-judging power of the death now approaching (v.31).
31:
"Now is the judgment of this world: now shall the prince of this world be cast out." The witnesses read Christ's death as the great crisis of the world and the overthrow of Satan.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the terms. Robertson: krisis, "a judgement" — "the next few days will test this world"; "the prince of this world" is Satan "in possession of the evil world" (John 14:30; John 16:11), who claimed the world in the temptation (Matthew 4:8-10). Barnes: the "judgment" relates to "something accomplished by that death," not the final assize, since he came "not to judge" (John 3:17). Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic "prince of this world."

The Reformed voice reads the victory. Calvin: "the Lord now, as if he had already succeeded in the contest, boasts of having obtained a victory not only over fear, but over death," describing "in lofty terms the advantage of his death" — the very thing that might have dismayed the disciples. Gill applies the judgment to the reproving and condemning of the unbelieving world by the Spirit through the gospel.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the dethroning: "now is the judgment given concerning it, whose it shall be. Now shall the prince of this world — Satan, who had gained possession of it by sin and death — be cast out, judged, condemned." So the witnesses agree: the cross, which looked like defeat, is in truth the world's decisive trial and Satan's eviction — the usurper cast down by the very death he engineered, and the throne of the world's prince broken so that the lifted-up Christ may draw all to himself (v.32).

So the witnesses read the cross, which looked like defeat, as the world's decisive trial and Satan's eviction: the usurper cast down by the very death he engineered. The throne of the world's prince is broken so that the lifted-up Christ may draw all to himself (v.32).

The cross that seemed defeat is in truth the world's decisive trial and the eviction of its usurping prince (v.32).
32:
"And I, if I be lifted up from the earth, will draw all men unto me." The witnesses read Christ's crucifixion as the magnet of the world — the lifting up that draws all.

The grammatical-historical readers fix "lifted up" and "draw." Robertson: hypsōthō, the same verb as the brazen serpent and the cross (John 3:14; John 8:28); the emphatic "I" set against the cast-out prince of v.31. Barnes: "will draw" is the word of John 6:44 — "I will incline all kinds of men... provide a way... the strongest inducement to all men to come." Ellicott: "the conqueror in opposition to the vanquished foe."

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the scope and the resistibility. Clarke: "after I shall have died and risen... I shall attract and illuminate both Jews and Gentiles" (Genesis 49:10). Wesley: "I will draw all men — Gentiles as well as Jews; and those who follow my drawings, Satan shall not" hold.

The Reformed voice reads the method. Calvin: "the cross will be, as it were, a chariot, by which he shall raise" men "from earth to heaven"; being lifted up, "he shall gather all men to himself." So the witnesses agree: the shameful lifting up on the cross is the very means of universal attraction — Jew and Gentile drawn to the crucified and risen Lord, the answer to the Greeks who would see him (v.21) and the counter-stroke to Satan's defeat; the Evangelist at once adds that "lifted up" signified the manner of his death (v.33).

So the witnesses read the lifting up as the magnet of the world: the shameful cross becomes the means of universal attraction, drawing Jew and Gentile to the crucified and risen Lord. It answers the Greeks who would see him (v.21) and counters Satan's defeat — and John at once fixes its meaning as the manner of his death (v.33).

The shameful lifting up becomes the very means by which Jew and Gentile are drawn to the crucified and risen Lord.

It answers the Greeks who would see him and counters the defeat of the world's prince in a single stroke (v.31).
33:
"This he said, signifying what death he should die." A brief editorial note, and the witnesses read John interpreting "lifted up" as the manner of the cross.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the comment. Robertson: sēmainōn, "to give a sign," the same phrase repeated at 18:32 and near it at 21:19; the indirect question with the imperfect "was about to." Ellicott: "the words are the Apostle's interpretation," remembered as twice recorded before (John 3:14; 8:28), here adding "from the earth" — a further thought not inconsistent with the others. Barnes cross-refers the saving design to Romans 5:18.

The verse is short because its work is precise: to fix, for any reader who might spiritualize "lifted up" into exaltation only, that Christ meant the literal crucifixion. Gill: "these are the words of the evangelist, interpreting the design of Christ."

So the witnesses agree: John, as elsewhere, supplies the key to a saying his Lord left in figure — "lifted up" is the cross, the specific, public, violent death by which the prince of this world is cast out (v.31) and all men are drawn (v.32). The Evangelist's habit of such notes (v.16; 18:32) is itself a witness to his care that the manner of the death not be mistaken; the cross is no metaphor but the literal instrument of the world's redemption, and the crowd, missing this, will object that they had heard the Christ abides for ever (v.34).

So the witnesses read John's note as the key to the figure: 'lifted up' is no mere exaltation but the literal cross, the public and violent death by which the world's prince is cast out and all men drawn. The Evangelist's habit of such notes (John 18:32) guards the manner of the death from being mistaken for metaphor.

The cross is thus no metaphor but the literal instrument of the world's redemption, fixed beyond mistaking by the Evangelist's note.

The Evangelist's care that the manner of the death not be mistaken is itself a witness to the centrality of the cross.
34:
"We have heard out of the law that Christ abideth for ever: and how sayest thou, The Son of man must be lifted up?" The witnesses read the crowd's objection — a half-truth of Scripture set against a dying Messiah.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the citation. Robertson: "out of the law" means out of the Scriptures (John 10:34; John 15:25), perhaps from Ps 89:36, 110:4, Isaiah 9:7, Daniel 7:14. Barnes: "the doctrine of many of them certainly was that the Messiah would not die, but would reign as a prince for ever." Ellicott notes the Rabbinic knowledge the Evangelist preserves. Lightfoot: "out of the law" is opposed to "the words of the scribes."

The Latin and Eastern fathers and the Puritan read the half-blindness. Augustine: they understood he spoke of his death and asked how that could be. Henry: "the people drew false notions from the Scriptures, because they overlooked the prophecies that spoke of Christ's sufferings and death." Chrysostom warns how "deceit is easily detected."

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the malice and the answer. Calvin: "their malice blinds them"; they pit the Messiah's perpetuity against his death "as if both statements had not been expressly made" in Scripture. Wesley resolves it plainly: "how can these things be reconciled? Very easily. He first dies, and then abideth for ever" (Ps 110:4). So the witnesses agree: the crowd holds a true text — Messiah abides for ever — but, blind to the prophecies of his suffering (Isaiah 53), they cannot reconcile it with a crucified "Son of man," and their "who is this Son of man?" draws from Christ a final urging to walk in the light while they have it (v.35).

So the witnesses read the crowd's objection as a true text half-understood: Messiah does abide for ever, but they are blind to the prophecies of his suffering (Isaiah 53), and so cannot reconcile a crucified Son of man with their hope. Wesley's resolution — 'he first dies, then abideth for ever' — meets their riddle, which draws from Christ an appeal to the light (v.35).
35:
"Yet a little while is the light with you. Walk while ye have the light, lest darkness come upon you." The witnesses read Christ's indirect answer — not argument but urgent appeal to use the closing day of grace.

The grammatical-historical readers note the turn. Robertson: "Jesus does not argue the point of theology with the crowd who would not understand," but returns to the metaphor of "the light of the world" (John 8:12), urging them to seize their privilege. Barnes: "he saw that they were offended by the mention of his death, and endeavoured to arrive at the same thing indirectly," warning the light would be with them "a little while." Ellicott: "it is not said that he answered them," for the reply was not direct.

The Reformed voice reads the rebuke and the threat. Calvin: "he gently admonishes... yet at the same time reproves them sharply," charging them with "shutting their eyes against the light," and "threatens that ere long the light will be taken away." Gill: he "suggests to them their ignorance and stupidity, amidst so much light."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke reads the image: Christ "compares himself to a light about to disappear for a short time," exhorting them "to follow this light while it was among them." Wesley: "the light — I and my doctrine." So the witnesses agree: to the crowd's riddling objection Christ gives no theological debate but a pastoral alarm — the light is leaving; walk in it now, before the darkness that follows refusal overtakes you, and so become children of light (v.36).

So the witnesses read Christ's reply as alarm, not argument: the light is leaving; walk in it now, before the darkness of refusal overtakes you. He meets their theological cavil not with debate but with pastoral urgency, pressing them to believe while the day of grace remains and so become children of light (v.36).

To the riddling objection Christ gives no debate but a pastoral alarm: the light is leaving, so walk in it now (v.36).

He presses them to believe while the day of grace remains, and so to become children of light (v.36).
36:
"While ye have light, believe in the light, that ye may be the children of light. These things spake Jesus, and departed, and did hide himself from them." The witnesses read the close of Christ's public appeal — and his withdrawal.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the call. Robertson: "believe in the light" means "believe in me as the Messiah" (John 8:12; John 9:5); "sons of light," a Hebrew idiom (Luke 16:8; 1 Thessalonians 5:5; Ephesians 5:8). Ellicott: "that ye may become sons of light," the most emphatic position. Clarke: that "the truth of Christ so dwell in and work by you that ye may be all light in the Lord."

The Reformed voice reads the heirs and the hiding. Calvin: he calls "sons of light" those who "like true heirs enjoy it to the end"; the withdrawal "relates to adversaries who burned with envy" — he hid himself from those eager to destroy him. Gill: an explanation of the preceding exhortation.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley sums the goal: "the children of light — the children of God, wise, holy, happy." Clarke notes the hiding was "either by rendering himself invisible" or by quietly slipping away. So the witnesses agree: with this last urgent call to believe and become children of light, Christ closes his public ministry to the nation and withdraws — the Light departing from those who would not walk in it; and the Evangelist now steps back to render his solemn verdict on the unbelief of the many (v.37).

So the witnesses read the verse as the close of Christ's public appeal to the nation: a last call to believe and become children of light, followed by his withdrawal — the Light departing from those who would not walk in it. The Evangelist now steps back to render his solemn verdict on the unbelief of the many (v.37).

With this last call the Light withdraws from those who would not walk in it, and the Evangelist renders his verdict on the unbelief (v.37).

The Light departs from those who would not walk in it, closing Christ's public appeal to the nation (v.37).
37:
"But though he had done so many miracles before them, yet they believed not on him." The witnesses read the Evangelist's grave summary of the nation's unbelief in the face of overwhelming evidence.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scope. Barnes: "this does not refer to any miracles wrought on this occasion, but to all his works" — "John here gives the summary or the result of all." Robertson: the imperfect, "they kept on not believing on him," "stubborn refusal in face" of the signs. Ellicott: "before them" means "in their presence," implying the earlier Gospels' signs "were well known."

The Eastern and Latin fathers and the Puritan read the wickedness. Chrysostom: "no small wickedness" to disbelieve such miracles. Augustine sets the unbelief against the foreordained who believed. Henry: "the honour done to our Lord Jesus by the Old-Testament prophets, who foretold and lamented the infidelity of the many that believed not."

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the design and the guilt. Calvin: lest any be "perplexed at seeing that Christ was despised," the Evangelist shows "he was supported by clear and undoubted testimonies," "but the blind did not behold the glory of God" in his miracles. Clarke: "they were blinded by their passions, and obstinately hardened their hearts against the truth." Wesley: the miracles were such "that they could not but see them." So the witnesses agree: the unbelief was not for want of evidence — the signs were many and plain — but the willful blindness of hearts that would not see; an unbelief so total that the Evangelist now shows it was foretold by Isaiah (v.38).

So the witnesses read the verse as the tragic summary of the ministry: not for want of evidence — the signs were many and plain — but through willful blindness, the nation believed not. The unbelief was so total that the Evangelist now shows it had been foretold by Isaiah, both lamented and, in a sense, foreseen (v.38).

The unbelief was not for want of evidence but the willful blindness of hearts that would not see, as Isaiah had foretold (v.38).
38:
"That the saying of Esaias the prophet might be fulfilled, Lord, who hath believed our report?" The witnesses read the first Isaiah citation (Isaiah 53:1), and at once raise the question of how prophecy and human responsibility relate.

The schools agree the prophecy did not compel the unbelief. Gill: "though this was not the end of these men in disbelieving... yet hereby they were eventually fulfilled"; the predictions "had no such influence on the wills of these men as to lay upon them a coactive necessity." Barnes: "this does not mean that the Pharisees rejected Christ in order that the prophecy should be fulfilled, but that by their rejection the same thing occurred which Isaiah foretold." Clarke renders hina as "thus" rather than "in order that."

The grammatical-historical Robertson is careful: "it is usually assumed that hina here... has its full telic force," probable "as God's design," "but it is by no means certain since hina is used... with the idea of result" as well as purpose.

The Reformed voice holds prophecy and certainty together. Calvin: "John does not mean that the prediction laid a necessity on the Jews"; Isaiah "uttered nothing but what the Lord revealed to him from the secret treasures of his purpose" — "it must have happened, though the prophet had not spoken of it." Wesley glosses "the arm of the Lord" as God's power in Christ's preaching and redemption. So the witnesses agree: Isaiah's lament that few believe "our report" was fulfilled in the nation's rejection — not because the word forced them, but because God foreknew and foretold the willful unbelief his power would meet; the next verse states the harder side, that they "could not" believe (v.39).

So the witnesses hold prophecy and responsibility together: Isaiah's lament that few believe 'our report' was fulfilled in the nation's rejection — not because the word forced them, but because God foreknew and foretold the willful unbelief his power would meet. The next verse states the harder side, that they 'could not' believe (v.39).

The prophecy foretold the rejection without forcing it, revealing God's foreknowledge of the willful unbelief his power would meet.
39:
"Therefore they could not believe, because that Esaias said again..." The witnesses read the harder word — a "could not" believe — and labour to keep it just.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers locate the inability in self-incurred hardening. Lightfoot: "they were not constrained in their infidelity"; rather "those things were true which the prophet foretold concerning them." Barnes: "can and could are often used... to denote the existence of such obstacles as to make a result certain" — as John 5:44, "how can ye believe which receive honour one of another." Clarke: "having resisted the report of the prophets, and the evidence of Christ's miracles, God" judicially withdrew. Robertson: "John is not absolving these Jews from moral responsibility, but only showing that the words of Isaiah had to be fulfilled."

The Reformed voice grants the harshness and the justice. Calvin: "this is somewhat more harsh," for taken plainly "the power of believing was taken from them"; yet "there is no absurdity in this," since "nothing could happen different" from God's eternal purpose, while the blame remains their own. Gill: "God had determined to leave them to the blindness and hardness of their hearts."
