The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley states the consensus: "by the just judgment of God, for their obstinacy and wilful resistance of the truth, they were at length so left to the hardness of their hearts" that no miracle could move them. So the witnesses agree: the "could not" is no innocent incapacity but a judicial hardening, the settled consequence of a willful rejection God foreknew and foretold — the inability that is itself the punishment of refusing the light (v.35).

So the witnesses read the 'could not' as judicial hardening, not innocent incapacity: the settled consequence of a willful rejection God foreknew and foretold. The inability is itself the punishment of refusing the light (v.35) — the schools differing only on whether to stress the self-incurred obstacle or the determinate purpose, all keeping the blame the sinner's own.

The inability is thus the punishment of refusing the light, the blame remaining, on every reading, the sinner's own (v.35).
40:
"He hath blinded their eyes, and hardened their heart..." The witnesses read the citation of Isaiah 6:10, and strive to read the divine blinding without making God the author of sin.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the quotation and soften the agency. Barnes: in Isaiah the command is "go, proclaim truth... and the effect will be, owing to the hardness of their heart, that their eyes will be blinded" — the preaching the occasion, the hardness the cause. Robertson notes the perfect "hath blinded" (tetyphlōken) and aorist "hardened" (2 Corinthians 4:4; 1 John 2:11; Mark 6:52), the quotation differing from the LXX. Ellicott observes the text is quoted three times in the New Testament — by Christ (Matthew 13:14), by John here, by Paul (Acts 28:26-27).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke guards God's justice: the words refer "more to the judgments that should fall upon them as a nation" than to their eternal state, and "to suppose that God was unwilling that they should turn... lest he should be obliged to save them, is" a slander on God.

The Reformed voice reads judicial abandonment. Calvin (on Isaiah 6:9): "I send thee to speak to the deaf" — God "intends to make" the people worse, as the just penalty of their obstinacy. Gill: whether God or the wickedness blinds, it is "not by any positive" infusion of evil but by withholding grace and giving them up. So the witnesses agree: the blinding is real and from God, but judicial — the righteous withdrawal of light from those who first refused it — not a cruelty that forbids repentance; the same Isaiah who pronounced it, the next verse says, had seen Christ's glory (v.41).

So the witnesses read the divine blinding as real yet just: the righteous withdrawal of light from those who first refused it, not a cruelty that forbids repentance. Quoted thrice in the New Testament (Matthew 13:14; Acts 28:26-27), it explains the mystery of unbelief without making God the author of sin — and the prophet who pronounced it had seen Christ's glory (v.41).
41:
"These things said Esaias, when he saw his glory, and spake of him." The witnesses read John's startling identification: the LORD whom Isaiah saw enthroned (Isaiah 6:1) was the glory of Christ — a strong proof of his deity.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the claim. Robertson: the correct reading is "because he saw his glory" — "Isaiah with spiritual vision saw the glory of the Messiah and spoke of him, whatever modern critics may think," as Christ said Abraham saw his day (John 8:56; Hebrews 11:13). Barnes: Isaiah "saw the LORD (in Hebrew, JEHOVAH) sitting on a throne"; "this is perhaps the only instance in the Bible in which Jehovah is said to have been seen by man" (Exodus 33:20), and John applies it to Christ. Lightfoot cites the Targum's "I saw the Lord's glory."

The Reformed voice reads the office and the deity. Calvin: "the prophet was not sent as a teacher to a single age," but "the glory of Christ was exhibited to him," that he might be "a witness of those things which should take place under his reign." Wesley states it boldly: "when he saw his glory — Christ's — and it is there expressly said to be the glory of the Lord, Jehovah, the Supreme God."

So the witnesses agree: the verse is one of the New Testament's clearest assertions of Christ's full deity — the enthroned JEHOVAH of Isaiah's vision is the pre-incarnate Son, whose glory the prophet beheld and of whom he spoke. The hardening word of v.40 was uttered by one who had seen the Christ in glory, so that the rejection of Jesus is the rejection of the very LORD of Isaiah's temple. Yet even amid this unbelief, the next verse notes, many of the rulers secretly believed (v.42).

So the witnesses read the verse as one of Scripture's clearest assertions of Christ's deity: the enthroned JEHOVAH of Isaiah's vision (Isaiah 6:1) was the pre-incarnate Son, whose glory the prophet beheld. To reject Jesus is thus to reject the very LORD of the temple — yet even amid this unbelief, many of the rulers secretly believed (v.42).
42:
"Nevertheless among the chief rulers also many believed on him; but because of the Pharisees they did not confess him, lest they should be put out of the synagogue." The witnesses read the secret believers — faith real but unconfessed for fear.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the surprising fact. Robertson: "in spite of what has just been said, many even of the rulers" believed (recall "the lonely shyness of Nicodemus," John 3:1ff); they "actually believed on him" in conviction. Barnes names "Nicodemus, Joseph, and others," the threatened penalty being excommunication (John 9:22-23). Ellicott: this is "strongly asserted in opposition to the spiritual blindness of the nation."

The Eastern and Puritan voices weigh the timidity. Chrysostom: "the love of human praise prevented the chief rulers" — a passion that "generates numerous sins." Henry: "they believed on him... but they did not do him honour enough, for they had not courage to own him."

The Reformed voice reads the mercy and the warning. Calvin: "amidst the general madness of the nation, there were many who were of a sound" mind — yet their silence under fear is no light fault. So the witnesses agree: even in the nation's deep unbelief God preserved a believing remnant among the rulers, but their faith, strangled by fear of man and love of human praise, dared not confess Christ — a half-faith the next verse weighs and finds wanting against the praise of God (v.43).

So the witnesses read the secret believers as a remnant God preserved amid the nation's blindness — yet a remnant whose faith, strangled by fear of man and love of human praise, dared not confess Christ. The next verse weighs this half-faith and finds it wanting against the glory that comes from God (v.43).

God preserved a believing remnant even among the rulers, yet their fearful silence is weighed and found wanting in the next verse (v.43).

Their faith, strangled by fear of man and love of human praise, dared not openly confess the Christ they had inwardly come to own, fearing the synagogue more than they loved the truth (v.43).
43:
"For they loved the praise of men more than the praise of God." The witnesses read the verdict on the secret believers — a faith real but ruled by the fear of man.

The grammatical-historical readers render it precisely. Ellicott: "for praise read in both instances glory — the glory which comes from men more than the glory which came from God" (cf. John 5:44, where Christ put it as a question). Barnes weighs the quality of their faith: "it does not appear that they had a living, active faith, but... a speculative acknowledgment" that "leads to no self-denial."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke lists "four obstacles of faith": "too great a regard to men; riches; the fear of disgrace; the love of the praise of men" — many "persuade themselves that they love God more than the world, till some trying" hour proves otherwise.

The Reformed voice names the guilt. Calvin: these men "were not guided by any superstition, but only endeavoured to avoid disgrace among men"; since "ambition had greater influence over them than the fear of God," their uneasiness "was no vain scruple of conscience" — "how great ignominy is incurred before God" by such fear. (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown here is off-topic.) So the witnesses agree: the secret believers' tragedy is that the love of human glory outweighed the glory of God — a misplaced fear that strangled confession and exposed a faith too weak to deny self; the chapter thus measures even belief by whether it will own Christ before men, as his own final cry will insist that to believe is to believe on the Father who sent him (v.44).

So the witnesses read the secret believers' tragedy as a misplaced fear: the love of human glory outweighed the glory of God, strangling confession and exposing a faith too weak to deny self. The chapter thus measures even belief by whether it will own Christ before men — the very thing his final cry insists upon (v.44).

The chapter thus measures even belief by whether it will own Christ before men, as his own final cry insists (v.44).
44:
"Jesus cried and said, He that believeth on me, believeth not on me, but on him that sent me." The witnesses read the opening of Christ's final public summary — faith in him is faith in the Father who sent him.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the setting. Robertson: "cried and said" is "probably a summary of what Jesus had already said"; here "John closes the public ministry of Jesus." Ellicott: the "cried" "forbids our understanding these words of any private discourse... it implies public teaching addressed to the multitude." Barnes: "believeth not on me" means "not on me alone, or his faith does not terminate on me" but rises to the Father.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the encouragement. Chrysostom: "because the love of human praise prevented the chief rulers from believing, Jesus cried... Why are you afraid to believe on me?" — for to believe on him is to believe on God. Augustine sets the believing "foreordained to eternal life" against the rest. Henry: "the honour Christ... asserted to himself, in the account he gave of his mission."

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the aim. Calvin: "to encourage his followers to a proper and unshaken steadfastness of faith," with "an implied reproof" of the cowardly fear of v.43; the loud cry "is expressive of vehemence." Wesley: "believeth not on me — not on me alone, but also on him that sent me: because the Father hath sent the Son, and because he and the Father are one." So the witnesses agree: against the timid half-faith just described, Christ cries aloud that to trust him is to trust God himself — there is no honouring the Father while refusing the Son; the epilogue that recapitulates his whole message (v.45).

So the witnesses read the loud cry as the answer to timid half-faith: to trust Christ is to trust God himself, for there is no honouring the Father while refusing the Son. The epilogue that follows recapitulates Christ's whole message to the nation, pressing the unity of the Son and the Father who sent him (v.45).
45:
"And he that seeth me seeth him that sent me." The witnesses read this brief saying as a claim of the closest union with the Father — and so of Christ's deity.

The grammatical-historical readers press the force. Barnes: "this verse is a strong confirmation of his equality with God. In no other way can it be true that he who saw Jesus saw him that sent him, unless he were the same in essence" — "of no man could it be affirmed," and to say it of Paul or Isaiah "would have been blasphemy," yet "Jesus uses this language familiarly and constantly." Ellicott: the word means "to behold, contemplate, gaze upon" — "he that beholdeth me beholdeth him that sent me," passing from the negative of v.44 to the positive.

The Reformed voice reads the comfort. Calvin: Christ "sends us to the Father" so as "to give true and thorough tranquillity to our consciences"; faith is firm "because it is stronger than the world, and is above the world." Gill: not a bodily seeing — many saw Christ as "a mere man" and were offended — but a seeing "with the eyes of the understanding" that perceives the Father's glory through him.

So the witnesses agree: to behold Christ with the eye of faith is to behold God, for the Son is the perfect image and revelation of the Father (John 14:9; Colossians 1:15). The verse seals the claim of v.44: there is no division between trusting the Son and trusting the Father, no seeing one without the other — the deity of Christ stated as plainly as anywhere, and the ground of the believer's settled peace. From this union Christ turns to his saving purpose as the light come into the world (v.46).

So the witnesses read the verse as the seal of v.44: to behold Christ with the eye of faith is to behold God, for the Son is the perfect image of the Father (John 14:9; Col 1:15). There is no seeing the one without the other — the deity of Christ stated plainly, and the ground of the believer's settled peace.
46:
"I am come a light into the world, that whosoever believeth on me should not abide in darkness." The witnesses read Christ's restatement of his saving purpose — the light come to deliver from darkness.

The grammatical-historical readers tie it to the chapter's metaphor. Robertson: "as in 3:19; 9:5; 8:12; 12:35" — "light dispels darkness," with the negative purpose clause "that he may not abide." Ellicott connects "abide in darkness" with becoming "sons of light" (v.36). Barnes: "darkness is put for error as well as for sin" (John 3:19; 1 John 1:5).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke reads the dawn: "before the coming of this Saviour, this sun of righteousness, all was darkness; at his rising the darkness is dispersed — but it only profits those whose eyes are open to receive" his rays.

The Reformed voice reads the assurance and the charge. Calvin: "to render his disciples more bold and persevering, he maintains the certainty of faith," testifying that he came "that men might be delivered from darkness and errors," and "points out the means" — believing on him; he "accuses of ingratitude all who, after having" the light, walk in darkness still. So the witnesses agree: Christ's errand is light — he comes to dispel the darkness of sin and error from all who believe; the gracious counterpart to the judicial blinding of v.40, and the ground of his next word that he came not to judge but to save (v.47).

So the witnesses read Christ's errand as light: he comes to dispel the darkness of sin and error from all who believe — the gracious counterpart to the judicial blinding of v.40. Only those whose eyes are open receive his rays, and the next verse adds that he came in this first advent not to judge but to save (v.47).

Only those whose eyes are open receive his rays, and he came in this first advent not to judge but to save (v.47).

It is the gracious counterpart to the judicial blinding of the unbelieving, light offered to all who will receive it (v.40).
47:
"And if any man hear my words, and believe not, I judge him not: for I came not to judge the world, but to save the world." The witnesses read Christ's present errand of mercy, holding back the judgment that will yet come.

The grammatical-historical readers note the text and the purpose. Robertson and Clarke prefer the reading "and keep them not" (phylaxē) over "believe not" — "a man must hear... believe... and keep"; the purpose clause, "but to save the world" (John 3:17). Barnes: "it was not his present purpose to condemn men. He would come to condemn the guilty at a future time"; now "he still gave them an opportunity to be saved." Ellicott cross-refers the apparent opposite, "for judgment I am come" (John 9:39).

The Reformed voice reads the mingled grace and severity. Calvin: "after having spoken concerning his grace... he now begins to strike the rebellious, though even here he mitigates the severity"; he "delays to pronounce on them, because, on the contrary, he has come for the salvation of all."

So the witnesses agree: in his first coming Christ is the Saviour, not the Judge — he withholds condemnation and presses the offer of life even on those who reject him. Yet the very mercy that delays judgment makes the rejection more solemn, for the word now offered in grace will itself become the standard of the judgment to come, as the next verse declares (v.48). The Light does not condemn; men condemn themselves by refusing it (John 3:19).

So the witnesses read the verse as the gospel of the first coming: Christ is Saviour, not Judge, withholding condemnation and pressing the offer of life even on those who reject him (John 3:17). Yet the mercy that delays judgment makes rejection more solemn, for the offered word will itself become the standard of the last day (v.48).

The very mercy that delays judgment makes the rejection more solemn, for the offered word becomes the standard of the last day (v.48).
48:
"He that rejecteth me, and receiveth not my words, hath one that judgeth him: the word that I have spoken, the same shall judge him in the last day." The witnesses read the solemn sequel — the rejected word becomes the judge.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the terms. Robertson: "rejecteth" (athetōn) is "to render null and void" (Luke 7:30; Mark 7:9); "that very word of Christ" will judge. Barnes: "he needs not my voice to condemn him. He will carry his own condemnation with him... his own conscience will condemn him; the words... will be remembered and will condemn him." Ellicott cross-refers John 3:18, John 5:45ff, and Hebrews 4:12.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke makes it concrete: "ye shall be judged according to my doctrine; the maxims ye have heard from my mouth shall be those on which ye shall be tried in the great day" (Matthew 25:31ff), acquitted or condemned as they obeyed or despised them.

The Reformed voice reads the threat. Calvin: "that wicked men may not flatter themselves as if their disobedience would pass unpunished, he adds a dreadful threatening, that though he were to do nothing in this matter, yet his doctrine alone would be sufficient to condemn them" — as Moses, in whom they boasted, would accuse them (John 5:45). So the witnesses agree: the grace that withholds present judgment (v.47) does not annul the reckoning — the very word offered in mercy stands as the measure of the last day, so that to reject the saving word is to choose one's own condemnation by it; and that word, Christ adds, is not his own but the Father's (v.49).

So the witnesses read the verse as the solemn sequel to mercy: the grace that withholds present judgment does not annul the reckoning — the very word offered in love stands as the measure of the last day. To reject the saving word is to choose one's own condemnation by it (Hebrews 4:12), and that word, Christ adds, is the Father's own (v.49).
49:
"For I have not spoken of myself; but the Father which sent me, he gave me a commandment, what I should say, and what I should speak." The witnesses read the ground of the word's judging authority — it is the Father's own.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the commission. Robertson: the perfect "hath given" — "Christ has permanent commission"; the indirect question keeps the deliberative subjunctive ("what I should say... and what I should speak"). Ellicott connects it with the Father's commandment of John 10:18 and the recurring claim (John 5:30; John 7:16-17; John 8:28).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke reads the disinterestedness: "I have not spoken for my secular interest... not set up myself, as your teachers do, to be supported by my disciples... I have taught you, not the things of men, but the deep, everlasting truths of God."

The Reformed voice reads the safeguard against a low view of Christ. Calvin: "that the outward appearance of man may not lessen the majesty of God, Christ frequently sends us to the Father"; since "it would be unlawful to transfer to another a single spark of the Divine glory," his doctrine "must have proceeded from God." (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown here is off-topic.) So the witnesses agree: the word that will judge the world is no human teaching but the very commandment of the Father given to the Son — which is why its rejection is so grave (v.48); and that commandment, Christ concludes, is itself life everlasting (v.50).

So the witnesses read the verse as the ground of the word's authority: what will judge the world is no human teaching but the very commandment of the Father given to the Son (John 10:18). This is why its rejection is so grave — and why Christ, lest his human appearance lessen God's majesty, sends us always to the Father, concluding that the commandment is life everlasting (v.50).

Lest his human appearance lessen God's majesty, Christ sends us always to the Father, whose commandment is itself life everlasting (v.50).
50:
"And I know that his commandment is life everlasting: whatsoever I speak therefore, even as the Father said unto me, so I speak." The witnesses read the climax and close of Christ's public teaching — the Father's commandment is itself eternal life.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the source. Robertson: "the secret lies in the source — as the Father hath said to me"; Christ's words are "spirit and life" (John 6:63), and Peter owned, "thou hast the words of eternal life" (John 6:68). Ellicott: "the Son speaks not of himself, but as executing this commission, which brings spiritual and eternal life to the world." Barnes: "he that obeys the commandment of God shall obtain everlasting life; and this is his commandment, that we believe on the name of his Son" (1 John 3:23).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke ties it to the gospel commission: "preach salvation to a lost world" (1 John 5:11-12); Wesley: "his commandment kept is life everlasting — that is the way to it, and the beginning of it."

The Reformed voice reads the appeal and the warning. Calvin: "he again applauds the fruit of his doctrine, that all may more willingly yield to it; and it is reasonable that wicked men should feel the vengeance of God, whom they now refuse to have as the Author of life." Gill distinguishes this "commandment" from the law, which promised only continued natural life. So the witnesses agree, and the chapter ends: the word Christ speaks is the Father's, and that word is life everlasting — to receive it is to live, to reject it is to be judged by it (v.48); the public ministry closes on the offer of eternal life through the Son who speaks only what the Father gives.

So the witnesses read the closing word as the chapter's last offer: the word Christ speaks is the Father's, and that word is life everlasting — to receive it is to live, to reject it is to be judged by it (v.48). The public ministry ends on grace: eternal life through the Son who speaks only what the Father gives (John 6:68).
